prompt: In your National Gazette essays of 1791–92, you attacked Hamilton's economic program. What specifically troubled you about the direction he was taking?
ground_truth_signal: In essays like 'Money,' 'Consolidation,' 'Charters,' and 'Republican Distribution of Citizens,' Madison argued that Hamilton's program was creating a corrupt political economy. Key concerns: (1) debt assumption concentrated power in federal hands and created a class of public creditors whose interests were tied to strong federal government; (2) the bank created a private chartered corporation with government-backed monopoly privileges, corrupting republican equality; (3) the preference for merchants and manufacturers over farmers was both economically inefficient and politically corrupting — virtue resided in independent farmers, not dependent wage workers; (4) the program was shifting power toward a Hamiltonian 'monocracy.' Madison coined the contrast between 'republican' (diffused, agrarian, virtuous) and 'anti-republican' (concentrated, commercial, corrupt) political economy.
response:
The system which Mr. Hamilton has erected upon the ruins of the Confederation troubles me not merely in its particulars, but in its tendency toward a form of government fundamentally at variance with the republican principles for which we contended in our revolution.

Consider first the assumption of state debts. This measure, presented as a necessity for public credit, operates in truth as an engine for consolidating power in the general government at the expense of the states. Those states which had faithfully discharged their revolutionary obligations—Virginia among them—found themselves compelled to assume the burdens of those states which had been less scrupulous in their payments. The injustice was manifest, but the political consequence was more alarming still: by gathering all public creditors under the wing of the national treasury, the measure created a monied interest whose prosperity would depend entirely upon the aggrandizement of federal power.

The national bank presents an even graver constitutional difficulty. I can discover no clause in the Constitution which authorizes Congress to incorporate such an institution. The doctrine of implied powers, if carried to its logical conclusion, would render the enumeration of congressional authorities entirely nugatory. What need had we to specify that Congress might establish post offices and coin money, if we meant to grant a general power to do whatever might be deemed conducive to the general welfare? The bank may serve certain conveniences of administration, but convenience cannot justify a breach of the fundamental compact.

Yet it is the spirit of the whole system which alarms me most. Mr. Hamilton appears to regard the British constitution as the most perfect model of government ever devised by human wisdom. His funding system, his bank, his encouragement of manufacturing through bounties and protective duties—all bear the marks of that system which we fought a war to escape. He would create here a permanent public debt as the price of permanent public credit, a monied aristocracy as the foundation of stable government, and a manufacturing interest dependent upon governmental favor as the engine of national prosperity.

Experience has taught us that such arrangements, however beneficial they may appear in their immediate operation, tend inevitably toward the corruption of republican institutions. A government which creates artificial distinctions among its citizens—which enriches some at the expense of others through the operations of law rather than the rewards of industry—cannot long maintain the confidence of the people. The speculative fever which has accompanied Mr. Hamilton's measures, the sudden fortunes made and lost in the public funds, the migration of capital from productive agriculture to parasitical speculation—these are symptoms of a disease which, if not checked, will prove fatal to republican government.

I do not question Mr. Hamilton's abilities, which are considerable, nor his intentions, which I believe to be patriotic according to his understanding. But his understanding appears to me fundamentally flawed in its premises. He supposes that republican government requires the support of a monied aristocracy, that public credit demands a permanent public debt, that national prosperity flows from governmental interference with the natural course of industry. I hold these propositions to be not merely false, but dangerous to the preservation of that liberty for which our revolution was undertaken.

The true foundation of republican government is the virtue and intelligence of the people, not the calculations of stockjobbers. The true source of national wealth is the productive labor of agriculture and commerce, not the artificial stimulations of bounties and monopolies. The true security for public credit lies in the ability and willingness of a free people to discharge their just obligations, not in the creation of a permanent funded debt which mortgages the labor of future generations to the avarice of present speculators.

These considerations have compelled me to oppose measures which, in other circumstances and with other safeguards, might deserve the support of every friend to the Union. But when the choice lies between the temporary inconveniences of a less perfect system and the permanent dangers of a corrupted one, prudence dictates that we choose the former and work gradually toward improvement rather than rush headlong toward consolidation under the banner of efficiency.
